skill.name: designing-architecture
skill.description: Guides software architecture design by matching project needs, team size, and system constraints to practical architecture patterns.
skill.summary: Select architecture patterns, project structures, and trade-offs for software systems.
skill.author: CloudAI-X

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences as external command execution and ordinary architecture text as weak cryptography. Manual review found only documentation templates, diagrams, and decision guidance in SKILL.md. No executable code, command invocation, cryptography, network access, filesystem access, or prompt injection was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged locations are fenced Markdown blocks containing checklists, ASCII diagrams, directory examples, and decision templates. They are documentation content and do not execute shell, Ruby, or other external commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown fence markers and static examples. No command interpreter, shell syntax intended for execution, or user-controlled execution path is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern in Plain Text
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography detector matched ordinary words and template labels in documentation. The skill contains no cryptographic API, algorithm selection, hashing operation, encryption routine, or key handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a single Markdown document about architecture design. The flagged lines contain metadata, headings, progress text, and option placeholders rather than cryptographic code.

content.value_statement: Architecture choices are hard to compare when project constraints are unclear. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through pattern selection, structure planning, and trade-off documentation.
actual_capabilities: Selects architecture patterns based on project size, team size, and system complexity.
Compares layered, clean, hexagonal, event-driven, CQRS, monolith, and microservice approaches.
Suggests feature-based and layer-based directory structures for application code.
Provides a trade-off template for documenting architecture decisions.
Uses a validation checklist to test whether the chosen architecture fits the project.
Encourages simple designs that can evolve as requirements grow.
limitations: Does not inspect a repository automatically unless the host agent provides code context.
Does not generate runnable infrastructure, deployment scripts, or application code.
Does not replace expert review for regulated, safety-critical, or high-scale systems.
Uses general architecture guidance and may need domain-specific constraints from the user.

use_cases.title: Choose a Startup Architecture
use_cases.description: Compare simple monolith, modular monolith, and clean architecture options for an early product.

use_cases.title: Plan a Growing Product Codebase
use_cases.description: Define module boundaries and directory structure before a medium-size team adds more features.

use_cases.title: Document a Technical Decision
use_cases.description: Create a clear decision record that explains options, trade-offs, consequences, and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Pick an Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose an architecture for a new project. The product is [describe product], the team has [team size], and the expected scale is [scale].

prompt_templates.title: Compare Pattern Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare layered architecture, clean architecture, and a modular monolith for [project]. Focus on complexity, testing, team fit, scalability, and maintenance.

prompt_templates.title: Design Project Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend a directory structure for [application type]. Include feature boundaries, shared modules, and rules for dependency direction.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Architecture Decision Record
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft an architecture decision record for choosing [option] over [alternatives]. Include context, options, trade-offs, decision, consequences, and validation criteria.

output_examples.input: We have a six-person team building a B2B SaaS product with complex billing and user management.
output_examples.output: A modular monolith is the likely starting point. It supports clear ownership for billing, users, and reporting while avoiding early distributed-system complexity.

output_examples.input: Should this small internal tool use microservices?
output_examples.output: The skill would usually recommend a simple layered application. It would note that microservices add deployment, observability, and coordination costs.

output_examples.input: We need to record why we chose event-driven processing for order fulfillment.
output_examples.output: The output would summarize the context, compare synchronous and event-driven options, list trade-offs, and define validation checks for reliability.

best_practices: Provide team size, project age, expected scale, and main business constraints.
Ask for explicit trade-offs instead of requesting a single pattern name.
Validate the recommendation against testing, deployment, ownership, and operational needs.
anti_patterns: Choosing microservices only because the system may grow later.
Copying a directory structure without defining module ownership.
Ignoring team skills when selecting complex architecture patterns.

faq.question: Can this skill choose between monolith and microservices?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares project size, team size, operational cost, and scalability needs before recommending an approach.

faq.question: Does it create production-ready code?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance, directory structure ideas, and decision documentation.

faq.question: Can it help with clean architecture?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains when clean architecture fits and how dependencies should flow toward the domain.

faq.question: Is this useful for small projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It often recommends simple layered designs for small projects and explains when to avoid extra complexity.

faq.question: Can it document architecture decisions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a decision template covering context, options, trade-offs, decision, and consequences.

faq.question: Does it review existing repositories?
faq.answer: Only if the host agent provides repository context. The skill itself is a Markdown guide.
